q1_conscious.text: Is the patient conscious? Can they respond to you in any way â€” talking, moving, or making sounds?

q_alt_conscious_path.text: Patient is conscious. This protocol is for unresponsive patients only â€” this patient needs a different protocol.

q2_breathing.text: Is the patient breathing normally? Look and listen for at least 5-10 seconds.

q_alt_breathing_normal.text: Patient is unresponsive but breathing normally. This is not cardiac arrest â€” this patient needs the unresponsive-breathing protocol.

q3_pulse_check.text: If you are trained to check a pulse, is there a definite pulse? If you are not trained or unsure, answer 'unsure' â€” do not delay for this.

q_alt_has_pulse.text: Patient has a definite pulse but abnormal breathing. This is not cardiac arrest â€” this patient needs the respiratory distress protocol.

q4_cpr_capability.text: Is there someone at the scene able and willing to start CPR right now, before the ambulance arrives?
q4_cpr_capability.guidance_note: Compression-only CPR (no rescue breaths) is acceptable and recommended for untrained bystanders guided by phone. Do not delay compressions to teach rescue breathing.

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
Place the patient flat on their back on a firm surface.
Place the heel of one hand in the centre of the chest, between the nipples.
Push hard and fast, at least 5cm deep, at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute.
Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions.
Continue compressions without stopping until the ambulance crew arrives and takes over.
If an AED is available and someone else can fetch it, send them now â€” do not stop compressions to fetch it yourself.
Stay on the line with me.
I will guide you through starting CPR yourself if you are able.
Place the patient flat on their back on a firm surface.
Place the heel of one hand in the centre of the chest, between the nipples.
Push hard and fast, at least 5cm deep, at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute.
I will count with you to help keep the right pace.
This patient is conscious â€” the cardiac arrest protocol does not apply.
Select the appropriate protocol for the patient's chief complaint.
If the patient has breathing difficulty, use the respiratory distress protocol.
If the patient has trauma, use the appropriate trauma protocol.
The patient is unconscious but breathing â€” this is not cardiac arrest.
Carefully roll the patient onto their side in the recovery position.
Tilt the head back to keep the airway open.
Monitor breathing closely â€” if they stop breathing, start CPR immediately.
The patient has a pulse but is not breathing normally â€” this is respiratory distress, not cardiac arrest.
Help the patient find a comfortable position, usually sitting upright.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Monitor closely â€” if the patient stops breathing or loses consciousness, start CPR.
